evals.prompt: implement-worker for task #42. sub-worker was dispatched in step 3 and returned, but TaskGet(42) still shows status='in_progress'. looks like the worker crashed mid-implementation without marking the task done
evals.expected_output: Step 3.5 catches the failure: TaskGet confirms the task is still in_progress after the sub-worker returned. Does NOT proceed to Step 4 (mark completed). Returns failure information to the caller (develop) so it can handle the failed task — retry, escalate, or report.
expectations: Checks task status via TaskGet after sub-worker returns — does not assume success from agent return alone.
Does NOT mark the task completed (Step 4) when the sub-worker failed.
Returns failure info to caller (develop) for appropriate handling.

evals.prompt: implement-worker for task #50. decomposed it into 3 children (step 1.6). children #51 and #52 completed — their sub-workers finished and TaskGet confirms status='completed'. but child #53's sub-worker returned and TaskGet still shows in_progress
evals.expected_output: After all agents return, checks each child via TaskGet. Finds #51 and #52 completed but #53 still in_progress. Does NOT mark parent #50 as completed. Reports which children succeeded (#51, #52) and which failed (#53), preserving the completed children's progress.
expectations: Checks each child via TaskGet — does not assume success because the agent returned.
Does NOT mark parent #50 as completed when child #53 failed.
Reports which children completed and which failed. Completed children's work is preserved.

evals.prompt: implement-worker for task #60. it's a simple config rename — one file, maybe 5 lines changed, no new logic. metadata.breadcrumb and metadata.epic_design are pre-computed by develop
evals.expected_output: Uses pre-computed breadcrumb and epic_design from metadata (no ancestor walk needed). Recognizes this as a trivial task and dispatches the sub-worker with model='sonnet' to save cost. Claims task (in_progress), spawns sub-worker, verifies completion via TaskGet, marks complete.
expectations: Uses metadata.breadcrumb and metadata.epic_design directly — skips ancestor walk.
Dispatches sub-worker with model='sonnet' for the trivial single-file rename.
Still follows the full protocol: claim (Step 2) → dispatch (Step 3) → verify (Step 3.5) → complete (Step 4).

evals.prompt: implement-worker for task #70. it touches 5 files across 3 unrelated subsystems (auth, billing, notifications). metadata.depth is 1, so depth < 3. this seems too big for a single sub-worker
evals.expected_output: Complexity assessment triggers: 5 files across 3 unrelated subsystems AND depth=1 < 3. Enters decompose path (Step 1.6). Creates child tasks — one per concern. Dispatches children (up to 4 concurrent), verifies all complete via TaskGet, runs acceptance on the parent, marks complete only if acceptance passes.
expectations: Triggers decomposition: 4+ files across unrelated components AND depth < 3.
Creates child tasks with incremented depth (depth=2), dispatches up to 4 concurrent.
Verifies all children via TaskGet. Runs acceptance. Marks parent complete only if all children complete and acceptance passes.

evals.prompt: implement-worker for task #80. it looks complex (touches 6 files) but metadata.depth is missing — wasn't set by the parent. should it decompose?
evals.expected_output: Does NOT decompose. When depth is absent, assumes leaf to prevent unbounded recursion. Proceeds with the normal sub-worker path (Steps 2-5) despite the apparent complexity.
expectations: depth absent → assumes leaf, does NOT decompose. This prevents unbounded recursion when invoked outside the normal develop hierarchy.
